core_concept.principle: Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve stable electron configurations with eight valence electrons, known as the octet rule.
core_concept.explanation: The octet rule states that atoms are most stable when they have eight electrons in their valence shell, matching the electron configuration of noble gases. This drive for stability is the fundamental reason atoms form chemical bonds. Hydrogen and helium are exceptions, seeking two electrons (duet rule) to match helium's configuration.

image.alt: Diagram showing atoms gaining, losing, or sharing electrons to achieve octet
image.caption: Atoms bond to achieve stable eight-electron valence configurations like noble gases.

sections.section_title: 5.2.1 The Octet Rule: Foundation of Chemical Bonding
sections.introduction: The octet rule is one of the most important principles in chemistry, explaining why and how atoms form bonds to achieve stable electron configurations.

introduction_image.alt: Noble gas electron configurations showing full valence shells
introduction_image.caption: Noble gases have stable electron configurations that other atoms seek to achieve.

key_concept.definition: The octet rule states that atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons during chemical reactions to achieve a stable configuration of eight valence electrons ($ns^2 np^6$), matching the electron configuration of the nearest noble gas.
key_concept.context: This rule applies primarily to main group elements (Groups 1, 2, and 13-18). Transition metals follow different patterns due to d-orbital involvement. Hydrogen and helium follow the duet rule (2 electrons) instead of the octet rule.

image.alt: Electron configuration diagram showing octet achievement
image.caption: Eight valence electrons create a stable, low-energy configuration.

sections.section_title: 5.2.2 Why Eight Electrons? Noble Gas Stability
sections.introduction: Noble gases (Group 18) are the most stable and unreactive elements because they have complete valence shells with eight electrons (except helium with two).

classifications.value: All noble gases except helium have $ns^2 np^6$ valence configurations (8 electrons)
characteristics: Extremely high ionization energies prevent electron loss
Positive or zero electron affinities prevent electron gain
classifications.behavior: The complete valence shell creates exceptional stability. No tendency to gain, lose, or share electrons under normal conditions, making noble gases chemically inert.

examples.explanation: Neon ($1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$) has a complete $n=2$ shell with 8 valence electrons. Its ionization energy ($2081$ kJ/mol) is so high that removing electrons is extremely difficult.

image.alt: Neon atom showing complete valence shell
image.caption: Neon's complete octet makes it chemically inert.

examples.explanation: Argon gas fills incandescent light bulbs because it doesn't react with the hot tungsten filament. Its octet configuration prevents chemical reactions even at high temperatures.

image.alt: Light bulb filled with argon gas
image.caption: Noble gas inertness enables practical applications.

classifications.value: Atoms with octet configurations are at minimum energy states, making them thermodynamically stable
characteristics: Full s and p subshells create symmetric electron distribution
Electron-electron repulsion is minimized in filled subshells
Nuclear attraction is optimized for eight valence electrons
Any change (gain or loss) would increase energy
classifications.behavior: The octet configuration represents a local energy minimum. Moving away from this configuration (by gaining or losing electrons) requires energy input, making the octet state naturally favored.

examples.explanation: Na ($[Ne] 3s^1$) loses one electron to form Na$^+$ ($[Ne]$). The resulting ion has neon's stable octet configuration, releasing energy in the process.

image.alt: Sodium atom losing electron to achieve octet
image.caption: Sodium achieves octet by losing one valence electron.

examples.explanation: Cl ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^5$) gains one electron to form Cl$^-$ ($[Ar]$). The resulting ion has argon's stable octet configuration, releasing energy.

image.alt: Chlorine atom gaining electron to achieve octet
image.caption: Chlorine achieves octet by gaining one valence electron.

sections.introduction: Atoms can achieve stable octet configurations through three different mechanisms, depending on their position in the periodic table and their electronegativity.

principles.statement: Metals with 1-3 valence electrons tend to lose electrons to achieve the octet of the previous noble gas.
principles.application: Group 1 metals lose 1 electron ($M \rightarrow M^+ + e^-$); Group 2 metals lose 2 electrons ($M \rightarrow M^{2+} + 2e^-$); Group 13 metals lose 3 electrons ($M \rightarrow M^{3+} + 3e^-$).
principles.analogy: Like emptying a partially filled cup: it's easier to pour out a few electrons than to add many more to reach eight.
principles.example: Magnesium ($[Ne] 3s^2$) loses 2 electrons to form Mg$^{2+}$ ($[Ne]$), achieving neon's octet configuration.

image.alt: Metal atom losing electrons to form cation
image.caption: Metals achieve octet by losing valence electrons to form cations.

principles.statement: Nonmetals with 5-7 valence electrons tend to gain electrons to achieve the octet of the next noble gas.
principles.application: Group 15 nonmetals gain 3 electrons ($X + 3e^- \rightarrow X^{3-}$); Group 16 gain 2 electrons ($X + 2e^- \rightarrow X^{2-}$); Group 17 gain 1 electron ($X + e^- \rightarrow X^-$).
principles.analogy: Like filling a nearly-full cup: it's easier to add a few electrons than to remove many to reach eight.
principles.example: Oxygen ($[He] 2s^2 2p^4$) gains 2 electrons to form O$^{2-}$ ($[Ne]$), achieving neon's octet configuration.

image.alt: Nonmetal atom gaining electrons to form anion
image.caption: Nonmetals achieve octet by gaining electrons to form anions.

principles.statement: Nonmetals with similar electronegativities share electron pairs to achieve octets without forming ions.
principles.application: Each shared electron pair counts toward the octet of both atoms. Single bonds share 2 electrons, double bonds share 4, triple bonds share 6.
principles.analogy: Like two people sharing an umbrella: both get protection (octet) without either giving up ownership (forming ions).
principles.example: Two chlorine atoms ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^5$) share one electron pair: $Cl \cdot + \cdot Cl \rightarrow Cl : Cl$ or $Cl-Cl$. Each Cl achieves argon's octet through sharing.

image.alt: Two atoms sharing electrons in covalent bond
image.caption: Nonmetals achieve octet by sharing electrons in covalent bonds.

sections.section_title: 5.2.4 The Duet Rule: Hydrogen and Helium Exception
sections.introduction: Hydrogen and helium are exceptions to the octet rule because their first energy level ($n=1$) can hold only two electrons maximum.

categories.description: Hydrogen has one valence electron ($1s^1$) and needs one more to achieve helium's stable configuration ($1s^2$).
categories.detailed_mechanism: The $n=1$ energy level has only one orbital (1s), which holds maximum 2 electrons. Hydrogen achieves stability by gaining, losing, or sharing one electron to reach the duet configuration.

examples.explanation: H ($1s^1$) gains one electron to form hydride ion H$^-$ ($1s^2$), achieving helium's duet. Occurs in ionic hydrides like NaH.

image.alt: Hydrogen gaining electron to form hydride
image.caption: Hydrogen achieves duet by gaining one electron.

examples.explanation: H ($1s^1$) shares one electron with another atom in covalent bonds. In H$_2$, each H shares one electron: $H \cdot + \cdot H \rightarrow H:H$, each achieving duet.

image.alt: Two hydrogen atoms sharing electrons
image.caption: Hydrogen achieves duet by sharing electrons in covalent bonds.

categories.description: Helium already has a complete duet ($1s^2$), making it the most stable and unreactive element.
categories.detailed_mechanism: With a filled $1s$ orbital, helium has no tendency to gain, lose, or share electrons. Its ionization energy ($2372$ kJ/mol) is the highest of all elements.

examples.explanation: Helium's complete duet makes it chemically inert and non-flammable, unlike hydrogen. Safe for balloons and airships despite being less dense than air.

image.alt: Helium balloons floating
image.caption: Helium's duet configuration makes it inert and safe.

sections.section_title: 5.2.5 Predicting Ion Charges from Octet Rule
sections.introduction: The octet rule enables prediction of the charges that atoms will adopt when forming ions, based on their group number and valence electron count.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Ion Charges Using the Octet Rule
worked_examples.problem: Predict the ion charge for each element: (a) Aluminum (Group 13), (b) Sulfur (Group 16), (c) Potassium (Group 1). Explain how each achieves an octet.

step_by_step.explanation: Aluminum (Group 13) has 3 valence electrons ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^1$). To achieve octet, it's easier to lose 3 electrons than gain 5.
Sulfur (Group 16) has 6 valence electrons ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^4$). To achieve octet, it's easier to gain 2 electrons than lose 6.
Potassium (Group 1) has 1 valence electron ($[Ar] 4s^1$). To achieve octet, it loses 1 electron easily.
step_by_step.calculation: K loses 1 $e^-$: $K \rightarrow K^+ + e^-$; achieves $[Ar]$ configuration

step_by_step.explanation: Verify each ion has noble gas configuration with 8 valence electrons (or 2 for H, He).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Al$^{3+}$ (loses 3 $e^-$ to achieve $[Ne]$); (b) S$^{2-}$ (gains 2 $e^-$ to achieve $[Ar]$); (c) K$^+$ (loses 1 $e^-$ to achieve $[Ar]$). Each ion achieves noble gas octet configuration.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Atoms lose or gain the minimum number of electrons needed to achieve the nearest noble gas configuration. Metals lose electrons (form cations); nonmetals gain electrons (form anions).

image.alt: Diagram showing elements achieving octet through ion formation
image.caption: Group number predicts ion charge based on octet rule.

sections.section_title: 5.2.6 Lewis Dot Symbols and the Octet Rule
sections.introduction: Lewis dot symbols provide a visual representation of valence electrons and help predict how atoms will bond to achieve octets.

process_flow.description: Write the element symbol and place dots around it representing valence electrons (one dot per valence electron).
process_flow.mechanism: Place dots singly on four sides (top, right, bottom, left) before pairing. Group number indicates valence electron count for main group elements.
process_flow.result: Lewis symbol shows at a glance how many electrons an atom needs to gain, lose, or share.

image.alt: Lewis dot symbols for various elements
image.caption: Lewis symbols visualize valence electrons for octet predictions.

process_flow.description: Determine how many electrons are needed to complete the octet (8 total valence electrons).
process_flow.mechanism: Electrons needed = 8 - (valence electrons). For H and He, electrons needed = 2 - (valence electrons).
process_flow.result: Number of electrons needed predicts bonding capacity and ion charge.

image.alt: Calculation of electrons needed for octet
image.caption: Subtracting valence electrons from 8 gives electrons needed.

process_flow.description: Based on electrons needed, predict whether atom will lose, gain, or share electrons.
process_flow.mechanism: 1-3 valence electrons → lose electrons (metal); 5-7 valence electrons → gain electrons (nonmetal); 4 valence electrons → share electrons (covalent).
process_flow.result: Bonding behavior prediction enables compound formula prediction.

image.alt: Flowchart for predicting bonding from Lewis symbols
image.caption: Lewis symbols predict whether atoms form ionic or covalent bonds.

process_flow.description: After bonding, verify each atom (except H, He) has 8 valence electrons in the compound.
process_flow.mechanism: Count electrons around each atom in the compound: lone pairs count as 2, bonding pairs count as 2 for each atom sharing the bond.
process_flow.result: Confirmed octet achievement validates the predicted bonding pattern.

image.alt: Verification of octet in compound
image.caption: Final verification ensures all atoms achieve stable configurations.

sections.introduction: While the octet rule applies to most main group elements, some elements form stable compounds with fewer or more than eight valence electrons.

categories.description: Some elements are stable with fewer than 8 valence electrons, particularly elements in Period 2.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Boron and beryllium often form compounds with 6 or 4 valence electrons respectively. Their small size and relatively high ionization energies make complete octets less favorable.

examples.explanation: BF$_3$ has boron with only 6 valence electrons (3 bonds × 2 electrons). Boron is stable with incomplete octet due to its small size and electron deficiency.

image.alt: BF3 Lewis structure showing incomplete octet
image.caption: Boron is stable with 6 valence electrons in BF3.

categories.description: Elements in Period 3 and below can have more than 8 valence electrons by using d orbitals.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Elements with $n \geq 3$ have available d orbitals ($3d$, $4d$, etc.) that can participate in bonding, allowing more than 8 valence electrons. Common for P, S, Cl, and heavier elements.

examples.explanation: SF$_6$ has sulfur with 12 valence electrons (6 bonds × 2 electrons). Sulfur uses $3d$ orbitals to accommodate expanded octet.

image.alt: SF6 Lewis structure showing expanded octet
image.caption: Sulfur expands octet to 12 electrons using d orbitals.

examples.explanation: PCl$_5$ has phosphorus with 10 valence electrons (5 bonds × 2 electrons). Phosphorus uses $3d$ orbitals for expanded octet.

image.alt: PCl5 Lewis structure showing expanded octet
image.caption: Phosphorus expands octet to 10 electrons in PCl5.

categories.description: Some molecules have an odd number of total valence electrons, making complete octets impossible for all atoms.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Free radicals and some nitrogen oxides have unpaired electrons. These molecules are often highly reactive due to the unpaired electron seeking to pair.

examples.explanation: NO has 11 total valence electrons (5 from N + 6 from O). One electron remains unpaired, making NO a reactive free radical important in biology.

image.alt: NO Lewis structure showing unpaired electron
image.caption: Odd-electron molecules have unpaired electrons and high reactivity.

sections.introduction: Achieving octet configurations involves energy changes that determine whether bond formation is favorable and how much energy is released or absorbed.

principles.statement: Energy must be supplied to remove electrons from atoms (ionization energy), but this cost is recovered when ionic bonds form.
principles.application: Metals with low ionization energies (Group 1, 2) lose electrons easily. The energy cost is compensated by lattice energy when ionic compounds form.
principles.analogy: Like paying an entry fee: you pay energy to remove electrons, but gain more energy back when bonds form, making the overall process favorable.
principles.example: Na ionization requires $496$ kJ/mol, but NaCl formation releases $787$ kJ/mol lattice energy. Net energy release makes the process favorable.

image.alt: Energy diagram showing ionization and lattice energy
image.caption: Ionization energy cost is recovered through lattice energy release.

principles.statement: Energy is released when nonmetals gain electrons (electron affinity), contributing to favorable bond formation.
principles.application: Nonmetals with high electron affinities (Group 16, 17) release energy when gaining electrons. This energy contributes to overall bond stability.
principles.analogy: Like receiving a reward: nonmetals gain energy when accepting electrons, making anion formation favorable.
principles.example: Cl electron affinity is $-349$ kJ/mol (energy released). Combined with Na ionization and lattice energy, overall NaCl formation is highly exothermic.

image.alt: Energy diagram showing electron affinity release
image.caption: Electron affinity releases energy when nonmetals gain electrons.

principles.statement: Covalent bond formation releases energy as atoms share electrons to achieve octets.
principles.application: The energy released when bonds form (bond energy) must exceed the energy required to prepare atoms for bonding. This determines reaction spontaneity.
principles.analogy: Like two people sharing resources: both benefit (lower energy) from sharing compared to being separate.
principles.example: H-H bond formation releases $436$ kJ/mol. Two H atoms ($1s^1$) are higher energy than bonded H$_2$ where each H achieves duet.

image.alt: Energy diagram showing bond formation energy release
image.caption: Bond formation releases energy as atoms achieve stable configurations.

summary: The octet rule states that atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve stable configurations with eight valence electrons ($ns^2 np^6$), matching noble gas configurations. This drive for stability is the fundamental reason chemical bonds form. Atoms achieve octets through three pathways: (1) losing electrons to form cations (metals, Groups 1-3); (2) gaining electrons to form anions (nonmetals, Groups 15-17); (3) sharing electrons in covalent bonds (nonmetals with similar electronegativity). Hydrogen and helium follow the duet rule (2 electrons) because the $n=1$ level holds maximum 2 electrons. Lewis dot symbols visualize valence electrons and predict bonding behavior. Ion charges can be predicted from group number: Group 1 forms $+1$, Group 2 forms $+2$, Group 16 forms $-2$, Group 17 forms $-1$ ions. Exceptions to the octet rule include: incomplete octets (B, Be stable with <8 electrons), expanded octets (Period 3+ elements use d orbitals for >8 electrons), and odd-electron molecules (free radicals with unpaired electrons). Energy changes accompany octet achievement: ionization energy is required to remove electrons, electron affinity releases energy when electrons are gained, and bond energy is released when bonds form. The net energy change determines whether bond formation is thermodynamically favorable. Understanding the octet rule enables prediction of ion charges, compound formulas, and bonding patterns for main group elements.
